examples: Backloading does not mean that your goods are treated in any way differently than on a normal journey.

definitions.definition: in a financial agreement, to arrange to pay more money at the end of an agreed period of time than at the beginning
examples: The bank allowed borrowers to backload mortgages, making minimal interest payments in the first few years, but at the cost of ballooning payments in future years.

definitions.definition: to arrange for an empty vehicle to transport goods on its return journey, usually at a reduced price
examples: If you backload your goods, it can mean a significant cost saving.

definitions.definition: the return journey of a vehicle that has taken goods to a place and left them there, or the goods that are transported on this journey
examples: Get your deliveries done as a backload or return job and start saving money.

definitions.definition: a large number of things that you should have done before and must do now
examples: I've got a huge backlog of work to do.

definitions.definition: a large bag used to carry things on your back, used especially by people who go camping or walking
examples: It's a frame backpack with a belt and padded shoulder straps.

definitions.definition: to travel while carrying your clothes and other things that you need in a backpack, usually not spending very much money and staying in places that are not expensive
examples: When we were students we backpacked in Thailand.
Gavin and I backpacked our way around the world.

definitions.definition: to walk from place to place in the countryside, carrying the things you need in a backpack and camping at night
examples: We backpacked in Sequoia National Park .
He'd backpacked the Smokies.

definitions.definition: a person who goes walking from place to place in the countryside, carrying the things they need in a backpack and camping at night
examples: "I'm a big backpacker," he says. "I love hiking in the Sierra Nevada Mountains."

definitions.definition: in, into, or towards a previous place or condition, or an earlier time
examples: When you take the scissors, remember to put them back.
He left a note saying "Gone out. Back soon."
She went to Brazil for two years, but now she's back (= has returned).
He looked back (= looked behind him) and saw they were following him.
Looking at her old photographs brought back (= made her remember) a lot of memories.
I was woken by a thunderstorm, and I couldn't get back to sleep (= could not sleep again).
The last time we saw Lowell was back (= at an earlier time) in January.
This tradition dates back to (= to the earlier time of) the 16th century.

definitions.definition: in return
examples: If he hits me, I'll hit him back.
You're not just going to let her say those things about you without fighting back, are you?

definitions.definition: in reply
examples: I'm busy at the moment - can I call you back?
I wrote to Donna several months ago, but she hasn't written back yet.

definitions.definition: further away in distance
examples: If we push the table back against the wall, we'll have more room.
"Keep back!" he shouted, "Don't come any closer!"
He sat back on the sofa.
She threw back her head and laughed uproariously.
The house is set back from the street.

definitions.definition: the inside or outside part of an object, vehicle, building, etc. that is furthest from the front
examples: He jotted her name down on the back of an envelope.
I found my tennis racket at the back of the cupboard.
We sat at the back of the bus.
Our seats were right at the back of the auditorium.

He put his jacket on the back of his chair (= the part of the chair that you put your back against when you sit on it).

definitions.definition: close together and facing in opposite directions
examples: The office was full of computers, and we had to sit back to back in long rows.

definitions.definition: happening one after another, without interruption
examples: Coming up after the break, three Rolling Stones classics back to back.

definitions.definition: with the back part of something where the front should be

definitions.definition: the side of your hand that has hair growing on it

definitions.definition: to risk money by saying that you think a horse, team, etc. will win a race, game, or competition in order to win more money if they do

definitions.definition: to (cause to) move backwards

definitions.definition: to cover the back of something with a material, often to make it stronger or thicker

definitions.definition: at or near the back of something

definitions.definition: the action of liquid splashing (= moving and falling) backwards or onto a surface that is near it

examples: Can you do backstroke?

examples: "All right," he backtracked, "It's possible that I was mistaken."

examples: Before we leave work each day, we make a backup of all the records we have entered into the computer that day.

definitions.definition: towards the direction that is the opposite to the one in which you are facing
examples: She left without a backward glance.
He did a fantastic backward somersault.

definitions.definition: not doing something immediately or quickly, because you are nervous or not certain about it
examples: I usually find that my partner is not backward in suggesting different options.

definitions.definition: → backwards mainly UK

a situation in which the price of a commodity (= a product, such as oil or a metal, that is traded in large quantities) is lower if the buyer is to receive the commodity at a future date than it is if the buyer receives it immediately
examples: Silver has been in backwardation for the past five weeks.

definitions.definition: the state of not being advanced, or of not using modern methods, technology, or machines
examples: They were accused of backwardness because they had no washing machine.
Local farmers won’t use donkeys because of their negative association with poverty and backwardness.

definitions.definition: towards the direction that is opposite to the one in which you are facing or opposite to the usual direction
examples: I walked backwards towards the door.

He began counting backwards: "Ten, nine, eight..."

definitions.definition: returning to older and less effective ways
examples: The breakdown in negotiations will be seen as a step backwards.

definitions.definition: first in one direction and then in the opposite one
examples: Paul paced anxiously backwards and forwards.

definitions.definition: the backward movement of waves, or the backward movement of water caused by something, such as a boat, passing through it
examples: The waterskier was caught in the backwash from a motorboat.

definitions.definition: an effect that is not the direct result of something
examples: The economic and political backwash of the war is still being felt.

definitions.definition: a part of a river where the water does not flow
examples: We tied the boat up in a quiet backwater overnight.

definitions.definition: a place that does not change because it is not influenced by new ideas or events that happen in other places

caused by, made from, or relating to bacteria

definitions.definition: a scientist who studies bacteria, especially those that cause disease

definitions.definition: unpleasant and causing difficulties

definitions.definition: of low quality, or not acceptable
examples: The plumber did a bad job on the repairs.

definitions.definition: not successful, or not able to do something well
examples: Business has been bad this year.

definitions.definition: damaging to health

definitions.definition: (of people or actions) evil or morally unacceptable

definitions.definition: not suitable or not convenient

definitions.definition: causing or experiencing pain

examples: Everyone at the conference wore a badge with their name on .
Don't forget to display your ID badge.

definitions.definition: a flat metal or plastic object, usually round, with a picture or message on it, that can be fastened to clothes with a pin, often in order to show that you like or support a particular group, idea, belief, etc.
examples: They used to go round wearing badges with slogans like "No nukes" and "Save the whale".

definitions.definition: A badged piece of clothing has a symbol or mark sewn onto it that shows which organization or group the person who wears it belongs to

definitions.definition: an animal with short legs, wide feet with long claws, and strong jaws, that lives underground and comes out to feed at night. There are six main types of badger. Most have narrow faces, heads with light and dark hair, and brown or grey hair on their bodies.
examples: The owl's predators include hawks, larger owls, snakes, and badgers.
We saw a fairly large black creature with a streak of white - a badger.

definitions.definition: to persuade someone by telling them repeatedly to do something, or to question someone repeatedly
examples: Stop badgering me - I'll do it when I'm ready.

definitions.definition: To beat something that is going to happen is to take action before the thing happens

definitions.definition: of the highest quality, or being the most suitable, pleasing, or effective type of thing or person

definitions.definition: in the most suitable, pleasing, or satisfactory way, or to the greatest degree
examples: Which evening would suit you best for the party?

definitions.definition: to the greatest degree when used as the superlative of adjectives beginning with "good" or "well"

definitions.definition: something that you cannot see clearly
examples: If I don't wear my glasses, everything is just a blur.

definitions.definition: something that you cannot remember or understand clearly
examples: It all happened so long ago that it's just a blur to me now.
The last few days seem to have gone by in a blur.

definitions.definition: to (make something or someone) become difficult to see clearly
examples: As she drifted into sleep, the doctor's face began to blur and fade.

definitions.definition: to make the difference between two things less clear, or to make it difficult to see the exact truth about something
examples: This film blurs the line/distinction/boundary between reality and fantasy.

definitions.definition: difficult to see clearly; making it difficult for someone to see things clearly

definitions.definition: to (cause something to) separate suddenly or violently into two or more pieces, or to (cause something to) stop working by being damaged
examples: The dish fell to the floor and broke.
Charles is always breaking things.
She fell and broke her arm (= broke the bone in her arm).
I dropped the vase and it broke into pieces.
I think I've broken your phone.
I picked it up and the handle broke off.
We heard the sound of breaking glass.

definitions.definition: to destroy or end something, or to come to an end
examples: Eventually someone spoke, breaking the silence.
She laughed and that broke the tension.
The enemy were unable to break the code (= understand it and so make it useless).
Outside workers were brought in in an attempt to break (= end) the strike.

definitions.definition: to do something better than the best known speed, time, number, etc. previously achieved
examples: She broke the record for the 5,000 metres.

definitions.definition: to fail to keep a law, rule, or promise
examples: He didn't know he was breaking the law (= doing something illegal).
She broke her promise/word to me (= did not do what she promised she would).

definitions.definition: to (cause something to) divide into two or more parts or groups
examples: These enzymes break down food in the stomach (= cause food to separate into smaller pieces).
I asked her to break her expenses down into food, travel and personal costs.

definitions.definition: to interrupt or to stop something for a short period
examples: We usually break for lunch at 12.30.
I needed something to break the monotony of my typing job.
The phone rang, breaking my concentration.
They decided to break their journey in Singapore.

definitions.definition: to go somewhere or do something by force
examples: He threatened to break the door down (= enter using force).
The horse tried to break free from its stable.
In the storm the boat broke loose from its moorings.
The thieves broke the safe open and stole the diamonds.
The police broke up the fight (= ended it forcefully).
She broke his grip and ran away.

definitions.definition: to lose your confidence, determination, or ability to control yourself, or to make someone do this
examples: He thought she would break under the strain.
They tried to break his will (= make him lose his control) but he resisted.

definitions.definition: to become known or to make something become known
examples: When the scandal broke (= came to the public's attention), the CEO resigned immediately.
It was the local newspaper that first broke the story (= told the public).

definitions.definition: (of waves) to reach and move over the beach, hit a cliff or wall, etc.
examples: A huge wave broke on/against the shore/over the boat.

definitions.definition: (of the weather) to change suddenly and usually become worse
examples: The forecast is for the hot weather to break today.

definitions.definition: (of a storm) to start suddenly
examples: We arrived just as a storm was breaking.

definitions.definition: When dawn or day breaks, the sun starts to appear in the sky early in the morning
examples: Dawn broke over the city.

definitions.definition: When a boy's voice breaks it begins to sound like a man's
examples: His voice broke when he was 13.

definitions.definition: If someone's voice breaks, it changes because of strong emotions
examples: Her voice was breaking with emotion as she pleaded for her child's return.

definitions.definition: (in tennis) to win a game in which another player is serving (= hitting the ball first)
examples: Nadal broke Cilic's serve in the second set.

definitions.definition: If a football team breaks, it gets the ball in its own half and attacks quickly into the opposing team's half
examples: Blues broke quickly, catching Wolves on the back foot.

definitions.definition: an interruption
examples: Finally there was a break in the rain and we went out.

definitions.definition: the short period of advertisements between television programmes

examples: a coffee break

definitions.definition: a place where something has broken

definitions.definition: If you are busy, you are working hard, or giving your attention to a particular thing
examples: Mum was busy in the kitchen.
The kids are busy with their homework.
She's busy writing out the wedding invitations.
I've got plenty of jobs to keep you busy.
He was too busy talking to notice us come in.

definitions.definition: If you are busy, you are not free or available to do something
examples: Would you like to come over at the weekend, or are you busy?
I'm busy on Tuesday morning but we could meet in the afternoon.

definitions.definition: A busy place is full of activity or people
examples: a busy restaurant
Their house is near a very busy road.

definitions.definition: In a busy period, you have a lot of things to do
examples: I have a busy week ahead of me.
You've had a busy day.

definitions.definition: If a phone line is busy, someone is using it
examples: I tried calling you but the line was busy.

definitions.definition: having too much decoration or too many colours
examples: The jacket was a bit too busy for my tastes - I'd prefer something plainer.

definitions.definition: to make the time pass by doing something
examples: I busied myself with tidying up my desk.

definitions.definition: to pay someone or do something for someone, so that that person does not tell anyone something that they know about and that you want to remain secret

definitions.definition: to allow someone to enter a place by pressing a button that opens a door and makes a buzzing sound
examples: Press the intercom and I'll buzz you in.

definitions.definition: to be busy and full of energy
examples: He predicts that Logan Airport will be buzzing as usual - or almost as usual - as Thanksgiving nears.

definitions.definition: If your head/mind is buzzing, you are thinking about many different things at the same time.

examples: The motorcycle was driven by a tiny bald man.

definitions.definition: used to show how something is done
examples: They travelled across Europe by train/car.

definitions.definition: near or at the side of

definitions.definition: during
